coredata.dc:title: A background correctable fluorescence sensing strategy for adenosine triphosphate evaluation based on aptamer configuration alteration
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Tingting
Ma, Fanghui
Huang, Feng
Zhang, Aomei
Yang, Minghui
Li, Xiaoqing
Chen, Xiang
coredata.dc:description: Ratiometric sensors with background correction are expected to get around the interference from complicated systems. However, there is currently a scarcity of viable carriers for quickly and easily assembling fluorophores with varying fluorescence emission. In this work, we developed a novel aptamer-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor for analysis of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) based on the properties of aptamer that enable conformational transformations upon binding to the target. The carbon dots (CDs) and thioflavin T (ThT) are employed as the reference and detection signal for the ratio-dependent sensor, which are both integrated with the aptamer. The green fluorescence of ThT was initially turn-on after binding with aptamer and then quenched in the presence of ATP, whereas the red fluorescence of CDs remained practically steady. This approach exhibits remarkable selectivity and can detect ATP in the range of 0.5–60 μM with a detection limit of 0.167 μM. Moreover, the method was successfully applied to the determination of ATP in human serum as well as in A549 cells, demonstrating the potential for medical diagnostic applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Adenosine triphosphate
Thioflavin T
Ratiometric fluorescence
Aptasensor

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of ratio-dependent fluorescence probe for ATP detection
Design and fabrication of CDs-ABA@ThT sensing platform
Design of the ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor
Sensitivity and Stability for ATP detection

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image with size distribution of CDs. (B) FTIR spectra of CDs. (C) UV/vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of the CDs (excitation wavelength: 425 nm). Inset: photograph of CDs solution under day light (left) and photoluminescence images (right) under UV light (365 nm) irradiation.

(A) The zeta potentials of CDs, ABA, CDs-ABA, ThT, CDs-ABA@ThT;(B) The fluorescence spectra of ThT, CDs-ABA@ThT in absence and presence of ATP, respectively. (C) CD spectra of the sensing system in the presence of different species in 50 mM Tris-HCl. The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.

Fluorescence emission spectra of CDs-ABA@ThT after reaction with different concentrations of ATP (0, 0.5, 1, 10, 20, 40, 60, 80, 100, 400, 800, 1000 μM) (inset: linear relationship between I0/I and ATP concentration). The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.
Selectivity for the detection of ATP (ATP, 100 μM; ATP analogs, 100 μM). The error bars represent the standard deviation of three measurements.
Box graph recording quantitative analysis of ATP concentration through CDs-ABA@ThT and ATP kit.
Preparation and application of CDs-ABA@ThT ratio-dependent fluorescence probe for ATP detection.
Recovery Assay of ATP in two Human Serum Samples by the ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor [34,35].

head.ce:title: A background correctable fluorescence sensing strategy for adenosine triphosphate evaluation based on aptamer configuration alteration

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: The Department of Dermatology, Xiangya Hospital, Central South University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Ratiometric sensors with background correction are expected to get around the interference from complicated systems. However, there is currently a scarcity of viable carriers for quickly and easily assembling fluorophores with varying fluorescence emission. In this work, we developed a novel aptamer-based ratiometric fluorescence sensor for analysis of adenosine triphosphate (ATP) based on the properties of aptamer that enable conformational transformations upon binding to the target. The carbon dots (CDs) and thioflavin T (ThT) are employed as the reference and detection signal for the ratio-dependent sensor, which are both integrated with the aptamer. The green fluorescence of ThT was initially turn-on after binding with aptamer and then quenched in the presence of ATP, whereas the red fluorescence of CDs remained practically steady. This approach exhibits remarkable selectivity and can detect ATP in the range of 0.5–60 μM with a detection limit of 0.167 μM. Moreover, the method was successfully applied to the determination of ATP in human serum as well as in A549 cells, demonstrating the potential for medical diagnostic applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluorescence sensors with background elimination function have received extensive attention because they can limit the effects of numerous interference elements including instrumental background signal variation and sample interference. Therefore, such sensor provide a dependable background adjustment mechanism that allows the output results to be determined only by the analyte, greatly improving the detection accuracy and specificity [1–3]. However, the preparation of the multi-emission fluorescence probe for ratio-dependent sensing is a key challenge, as it requires integrating multiple fluorescence groups with the same excitation, but with different emission wavelengths. To address this challenge, aptamers could be considered to simplify the process.

Aptamers are short nucleotide sequences that can bind with targets with high specificity and affinity by folding into complex 3D structures [4,5]. One example being single-stranded DNA with guanine (G)-rich sequence forming a secondary structure via intramolecular hydrogen-bonding interactions, referred to as a G-quadruplex [6,7]. Its formation is known to be promoted by the presence of monovalent cations and organic dyes such as sodium, thallium ions, SYBR Green I, and thioflavin T(ThT) [8,9]. Aptamers offer a number of potential advantages over conventional antibodies, including large-scale synthesis, low cost, minimal batch-to-batch variation, low immunogenicity and easy of chemical modification [10,11]. Based on the advantages, aptamers exhibit great flexibility in the design of fluorescence sensors. For example, Song et al. designed a turn-on fluorescent aptasensor for label-free detection of ATP by utilizing the in-situ formation of copper nanoparticles (Cu NPs) and the specific digestion capability of exonuclease I (Exo I) [12]. In addition, owing to the presence of phosphate groups, aptamers are negatively charged and are able to bind with positively charged fluorophores via electrostatic interactions. For example, Pan et al. reported a ratio-dependent luminescence aptasensor that employed a positively charged dual emission luminous nanoparticles (PLNP) to attach to aflatoxin B1 aptamers for determination of AFB1 in food samples [1].

Inspired by these strategies, we designed an aptamer-based ratiometric fluorescence approach that makes use of the features of aptamers, which enable conformational transformations upon binding to the target. On the one hand, by producing structural changes of the aptamer due to the presence of target, the emission intensity of the fluorophore bound to the aptamer can be employed as a detection probe. The emission intensity of another fluorescent substance attached to the aptamer by electrostatic attraction, on the other hand, remains practically unchanged throughout the procedure, serving as a reference signal probe. To verify the performance of the scheme, adenosine triphosphate (ATP) was selected as the model analyte target. As the core element of an organism's biochemical system, ATP regulates various biochemical pathways as well as cellular metabolic processes, and can serve as an indicator of cellular viability and cellular-damage [13]. Accordingly, evaluating ATP levels is of significant importance for biochemical research and clinical diagnostics.

ATP-binding aptamer (ABA) is a G-rich single-stranded DNA oligonucleotides that can form an intramolecular G-quadruplex structure. As a functional commercial dye, ThT has been shown to be an efficient inducer for G-quadruplex DNAs over other DNA forms [9]. ThT emits negligible fluorescent signal in aqueous solution, however, once combined to the G-quadruplex DNAs, ThT emits strong fluorescence signal in the visible region [14,15]. This property propels ThT to become the preferred fluorescent reporter for ATP label-free aptamer sensors [16,17]. Carbon dots (CDs) have been applied to the fluorescence detection due to their interesting fluorescence properties, simple synthetic routes, and good biocompatibility [18–22]. Positively charged CDs were selected to bind to ABA and their fluorescence signal was used as the reference signal component of the ratio-dependent fluorescence sensor to correct background.

Herein, a novel ratio-dependent fluorescence sensor based on aptamer is prepared that can provide sensitive ATP determination utilizing CDs as the reference signal and ThT as the detection signal. Both CDs and ThT are integrated with ABA. The fluorescence of ThT will be turned on after binding with ABA, and then decreased with the presence of ATP. However, the fluorescence of CDs remains almost constant during the detection process. The ratio of fluorescence intensity of ThT to CDs can be used for the assaying of ATP. The CDs-ABA@ThT sensor platform can specifically recognize ATP in human serum and cancer cells, demonstrating its efficiency in the quantitative determination of ATP clinically. This strategy not only make it easier to assemble fluorophores based on aptamers but also lessen the interference. Moreover, this strategy can be potentially applied to detection of other targets, requiring only replacing the corresponding aptamer systems.

Neutral red (C15H17ClN4, >90%), ethylene glycol (C2H6O2, 99.99%), ethanol, acetone, hexane and ThT were acquired from Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Adenosine triphosphate (ATP), thymine triphosphate (TTP), cytidine triphosphate (CTP), and uridine triphosphate (UTP) were purchased from Sango Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK8) and RPMI 1640 medium were bought from Dingguo Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Changsha, China). The ATP kit was got from Imel Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Changsha, China). The human serum was obtained from the Xiangya Hospital. Ultrapure water (18 MΩ·cm) was used throughout the experiment. Phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) solution (10 mM, pH=7.4) and Tris-HCl buffer solution (50 mM Tris, 100 mM NaCl and 10 mM MgCl2, pH=7.4) were used as the reaction buffers. Amino-modified ABA was synthesized and purified with HPLC by Sango Biotechnology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The sequence is as follows:
5′-NH2-(CH2)6-ACCTGGGGGAGTAT TGCGGAGGAAGGT-3′

All fluorescence spectra were recorded on a F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Fourier transform infrared spectra (FTIR) were recorded on a NICOLET iS20 FTIR spectrophotometer (Thermo, USA). UV–vis absorption characterizations were performed using a UV-2450 spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan). Transmission electron microscopic (TEM) images were collected on a JEM-F200 transmission electron microscope (JEOL, Japan). Zeta potential were measured on a Nano-ZSE Zetasizer (Malvern, U.K.) The fluorescence images were obtained on an inverted fluorescence microscope (IX 83, Olympus, Japan). The circular dichroism (CD) spectra were collected by a circular dichroism spectrometer (J-815, Japan).

CDs were synthesized according to reported method with little modifications [23]. Neutral red (2 g) was mixed with glycol (50 mL) and stirred for 15 min, then the solution was heated in a 100 mL Teflon-lined autoclave at 200 °C for 4 h. After cooled to room temperature naturally, 50 mL of acetone was added to the resulting solutions prior to sonication. Then, the collected sediment was centrifuged at 12000 rpm for three times in hexane and alcohol (v/v, 5%). Finally, the CDs were resuspended in 5 mL PBS for subsequent experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of ratio-dependent fluorescence probe for ATP detection
ce:section.ce:para: To prepare ATP aptamer-based multi-emission fluorescence probe for ATP determination, the ABA solution was firstly heated for 5 min at 95 °C to dissociate intermolecular interaction, and then rapidly cooled in ice water. Then, 100 µL of CDs dispersion and 100 µL of ABA solution (3 µM) were transferred into a 0.5 mL of centrifuge tube. ABA modified CDs (CDs-ABA) was obtained by fully shaking the mixed solution at room temperature for 30 min. Following that, 100 µL of ThT solution was added to the aforesaid solution and incubated at 37 ℃ for 10 min to give ThT functionalized CDs-ABA (CDs-ABA@ThT) as the ratio-dependent fluorescence probe for ATP detection.

A series of 100 µL of ATP solutions with varying concentrations (0–1000 µM) were added to the 100 µL of the probe solution, which was then incubated at 37 °C for 40 min. Finally, the fluorescence emission spectra were recorded in the range of 450–800 nm (λ ex = 425 nm).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design and fabrication of CDs-ABA@ThT sensing platform
ce:section.ce:para: The CDs-ABA@ThT sensing platform is designed based on CDs and ThT integrated with ABA ( Scheme 1). The CDs and ThT are separately employed as the reference and detection signal of the ratiometric sensor. As shown in Scheme 1, to achieve high specificity and simplicity of the ratiometric fluorescence sensor, ABA was electrostatically connected with CDs to produce CDs-ABA. ThT induces the formation of a more stable G-quadruplex structure through binding to ABA (CDs-ABA@ThT). In the presence of ATP, the ABA preferentially binds to ATP, leading CDs-ABA@ThT to release ThT, which causes the fluorescence at 498 nm of ThT decrease. However, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 629 nm keeps constant, which employed as a reference signal.

CDs are prepared through one-step hydrothermal method using neutral red and ethylene glycol as the precursors [23]. Morphology and particle size of the CDs are observed by TEM. Fig. 1A displays that the CDs are monodispersed and spherically shaped. In addition, the CDs are uniformly sized with an average value of around 3.6 nm. As indicated by the FTIR spectrum of CDs (Fig. 1B), the peaks at 3500–3000 cm−1 correspond to the stretching vibrations of O-H and N-H. In addition, the peak at 1424 cm−1 and 1045 cm−1 ascribed to the bending modes of N-H and the stretching modes of C-OH. The peaks at 2990–2850 cm−1 correspond to the C-H stretching modes. Bending vibrations peaks of C-H at 1371 cm−1 and 885 cm−1 are also observed [24,25]. The characteristic absorption peak of CO stretching vibration can be observed at 1700 cm−1 [26,27]. Peaks at 1236 cm−1 and 1641 cm−1 related to typical stretching modes of C-C and CN. UV–vis absorption and fluorescence emission spectra were used to explore the optical properties of CDs. As a result of trapping of excited-state energy of the surface states, the sp2 conjugating chromophore of the CO and CN bonds undergo an n-π * transition [28,29]. This is verified by the two absorption bands between 400–600 nm observed in Fig. 1C, which are the source of the strong fluorescence intensity produced by the CDs. The maximum excitation wavelength of the CDs was 450 nm as shown in Fig. 1C. However, during the following experimental, excitation wavelength of 425 nm was selected as 425 nm was also the maximum excitation wavelength of ThT. When excited at 425 nm, the CDs display a single peak emission in the range of 560–800 nm, with the maximum emission wavelength located at around 629 nm. It complies with the experiment's requirements and distinguishes itself clearly from the ThT fluorescence signal.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Design of the ratiometric fluorescence aptasensor
ce:section.ce:para: To investigate whether the CDs-ABA@ThT conjugates were successfully prepared, a series of tests were performed. As can be seen from Fig. 2A, upon addition of ABA, the zeta potential of CDs reduced significantly to a strong negative potential, indicating the positively-charged amino groups on CDs can combine with ABA via electrostatic interaction. In addition, the hydroxyl groups on the surface of CDs would interact with the amino groups at the 5′ end of ABA through hydrogen bonding, leading to a stronger bond between CDs and ABA. However, after CDs-ABA mixed with ThT, no obvious change in the zeta potential of the CDs-ABA was observed, showing that the addition of ThT has no effect on the binding of CDs with ABA. Fig. 2B shows the fluorescence spectra of ThT and CDs-ABA@ThT in absence and presence of ATP, respectively. When ThT is present in the solvent alone, only a small fluorescence peak is observed at 498 nm (excitation: 425 nm). However, a dramatically enhanced fluorescence of ThT and the red fluorescence of CDs (emission: 629 nm) can be observed for the CDs-ABA@ThT. After incubation with ATP (1 mM) for 40 min, the fluorescence intensity of ThT decreased significantly. In contrast, the fluorescence intensity of CDs was found to be almost unchanged. All the above results indicated that the CDs-ABA@ThT conjugates were successfully prepared, providing foundation for ATP qualitative detection.

The circular dichroism (CD) characterization was performed to characterize the change of secondary structure of ABA. From Fig. 2C, the CD spectrum of ABA exhibits a typical parallel G-quadruplex structure (the native peak at 240 nm and the positive peak at 265 nm) [30]. After ABA binding to CDs, CD signal was enhanced probably because the positively charged CDs can induce the formation of G-quadruplex structure. In the presence of ThT (20 µM), the CD signal was enhanced again, indicating that more and more G-quadruplex was formed. This phenomenon is consistent with the results that fluorescence spectral intensity of ThT is significantly increased after binding with ABA. However, when there was 2 mM ATP in the system, the positive peak at 265 nm decreased and the negative peak at 240 nm directly displaced by the strong negative CD signal at 245 nm, which has been shown to be the characteristic peak of ATP [16]. It shows that the G-quadruplexes structures of ABA were destroyed after ATP competitively replace ThT from the CDs-ABA@ThT. The CD assay results once again confirmed the ratiometric fluorescence sensor was successfully prepared.

The concentrations of ABA, ThT and the reaction time of CDs-ABA@ThT with ATP were optimized for the CDs-ABA@ThT based ATP detection system. The fluorescence quenching efficiency is expressed by I0/I, where I and I0 are the ratio of fluorescence emission peak intensities of ThT (I498) and CDs (I629) in the presence and absence of ATP. As the probe carrier, the concentration of ABA plays a critical role. Fig. S1 displays the highest I0/I was obtained when 3 μM ABA was applied. The concentration of ThT also plays an essential role, as it influences the performance for ATP determination significantly. As can be seen in Fig. S2, the optimized concentration of ThT is 20 μM. As another key factor, for the reaction time of CDs-ABA@ThT with ATP, the I0/I reached the maximum value with 40 min and reduced with longer reaction time, indicating the optimum reaction time is 40 min (Fig. S3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensitivity and Stability for ATP detection
ce:section.ce:para: ATP with various concentrations were mixed with CDs-ABA@ThT under the optimal conditions. As the concentration of ATP increased from 0 to 1000 μM ( Fig. 3), the fluorescence intensity of ThT gradually decreased, whereas CDs fluorescence intensity remained stable. The quenching efficiency was defined as I0/I. As shown in the inset of Fig. 3, the fitting curve shows a good linear relationship between I0/I value and ATP concentration in the range of 0.5–60 μM. The linear fitting equation is y = 1.091 + 0.014x with a linear correlation coefficient of 0.996, and the limit of detection was calculated to be 0.167 μM (3δ/s, where δ is the relative standard deviation of the blank solution and s is the slope of the fitting curve [31,32], n = 11). Obviously, this method has a wider linear range and a lower detection limit than most of previous reports methods for ATP detection (Table S1). Then, the stability of the CDs-ABA@ThT for ATP determination was studied. As shown in Fig. S4, when the CDs-ABA@ThT was stored for 120 h, the detection results for ATP was still comparable to 120 h before, indicating the good stability of CDs-ABA@ThT for ATP determination. The above results show the ratio-dependent fluorescence strategy in this work has the advantages of high sensitivity, good stability and simple operation.

High specificity toward ATP is essential for application of the assay in real sample analysis. In this work, the selectivity of the CDs-ABA@ThT ratio-dependent fluorescence probe for ATP was assessed by investigating the interference of other ATP analogs, such as TTP, CTP, and UTP. As displayed in Fig. 4, for the same concentration of these compounds (100 μM), only ATP resulted fluorescence quenching of CDs-ABA@ThT, demonstrating good selectivity for ATP analysis.

To demonstrate the applicability of the sensor for quantification of ATP in real samples, recovery measurements were carried out in two healthy human serum samples by the standard addition method. Different concentrations of ATP (5,10 and 40 μM) standard solution were added into 1% human serum and then analyzed ( Table 1). The recoveries are in the range of 96.4%− 104.2% with RSD from 0.4–2.8%, which indicated that the sensor can realize precise detection of ATP in complicated clinical samples. In addition, further experiments were performed to analyze cellular ATP in A549 extractions. As Fig. 5 shows, the concentration of ATP detected by our sensor (50.89 ± 1.98 μM) was in consistent with those obtained by ATP kit (52.13 ± 1.39 μM). Meanwhile, we found that when A549 and Hela cells were incubated with different concentrations (0.025, 0.05, 0.1 μM) of CDs-ABA@ThT probes at 37 °C for 8 h, cell survival was 94.7%− 97.5% and 94.7%− 99.1%, respectively, which indicated the CDs-ABA@ThT probes exhibited good biocompatibility to A549 and Hela cells (Fig. S5). A549 and Hela cells were incubated with different concentrations of CDs-ABA@ThT probes (0, 0.1 and 0.2 μM) for 6 h at 37 °C to explore the potential of CDs-ABA@ThT probes as tumor cell imaging reagent. Fig. S6 shows the fluorescent inverted images of A549 and Hela cells, in which cells show obvious red luminescence of CDs. This result indicated that CDs-ABA@ThT successfully entered the cells after incubation and it has the potential to perform intracellular ATP imaging. Meanwhile, the green luminescence of ThT was not observed because the fluorescence of thioflavin was quenched due to the high intracellular ATP level [33]. These above results demonstrated that this ratiometric fluorescence sensing strategy is reliable and available for ATP detection in complex biological samples.

In conclusion, with the merits of simple, low-cost, high selectivity and sensitivity, this work designed a new concept for construction of ratio-dependent fluorescent sensor for ATP determination. The ratio-dependent fluorescence analysis and imaging of ATP were effectively carried out using the CDs-ABA@ThT nanoprobe, which not only increased the sensitivity for low abundance ATP but also considerably decreased background interference. The sensing platform was constructed using an aptamer as the carrier, which greatly simplified the experimental procedures and improved the fluorophore loading. In view of the excellent detection performance for ATP, this method can be potentially applied to detection for other targets, requiring only replacing the corresponding aptamer systems.

Zhang Aomei: Data curation. Huang Feng: Formal analysis, Investigation, Software. Ma Fanghui: Conceptualization, Methodology. Wang Tingting: Conceptualization, Data curation, Writing – original draft. Li Xiaoqing: Project administration, Writing – review & editing. Yang Minghui: Funding acquisition, Project administration, Supervision. Chen Xiang: Methodology, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful for the support of this work by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (Grant No. 22174163), the Hunan Provincial Science and Technology Plan Project, China (No. 2019TP1001), and the Project of Intelligent Management Software for Multimodal Medical Big Data for New Generation Information Technology, Ministry of Industry and Information Technology of People’s Republic of China (TC210804V).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.snb.2024.135306.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
Supplementary material
.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Ratiometric luminescence aptasensor based on dual-emissive persistent luminescent nanoparticles for autofluorescence- and exogenous interference-free determination of trace aflatoxin B1 in food samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L.-M. Pan, X. Zhao, X. Wei, L.-J. Chen, C. Wang, X.-P. Yan, Ratiometric Luminescence Aptasensor Based on Dual-Emissive Persistent Luminescent Nanoparticles for Autofluorescence- and Exogenous Interference-Free Determination of Trace Aflatoxin B1 in Food Samples, Anal. Chem. 89 (2022) 8816–8821. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.analchem.2c00861.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A signal on-off ratiometric electrochemical sensor coupled with a molecular imprinted polymer for selective and stable determination of imidacloprid

Chen, A signal on-off ratiometric electrochemical sensor coupled with a molecular imprinted polymer for selective and stable determination of imidacloprid, Biosens Bioelectron. 154 (2020) 112091. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.bios.2020.112091.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: A smartphone-integrated ratiometric fluorescence sensor for visual detection of cadmium ions

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Analysis of aptamer discovery and technology

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Aptamer-based detection of circulating targets for precision medicine

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Wu, Y. Wang, X. Xu, Y. Liu, B. Lin, M. Zhang, J. Zhang, S. Wan, C. Yang, W. Tan, Aptamer-Based Detection of Circulating Targets for Precision Medicine, Chem. Rev. 121 (2021) 12035–12105. https://doi.org/10.1021/acs.chemrev.0c01140.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: G-Quadruplexes: prediction, characterization, and biological application

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C.K. Kwok, C.J. Merrick, G-Quadruplexes: Prediction, Characterization, and Biological Application, Trends Biotechnol. 35 (2017) 997–1013. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.tibtech.2017.06.012.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent amplifying recognition for DNA G-quadruplex folding with a cationic conjugated polymer: a platform for homogeneous potassium detection

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Effects of monovalent cations on folding kinetics of G-quadruplexes

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Thioflavin T as an efficient inducer and selective fluorescent sensor for the human telomeric G-quadruplex DNA

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: J. Mohanty, N. Barooah, V. Dhamodharan, S. Harikrishna, P.I. Pradeepkumar, A.C. sb:title.sb:maintitle: Three decades of nucleic acid aptamer technologies: lessons learned, progress and opportunities on aptamer development

ce:biography.ce:simple-para: Tingting Wang is currently studying for her MS degree at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University. Her research area is fluorescence sensors.
Fanghui Ma is currently studying for her Ph.D degree at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University. Her research area include fluorescence sensors and biosensors.
Feng Huang is currently studying for her MS degree at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University. Her research area is fluorescence sensors.
Aomei Zhang is currently studying for her MS degree at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University. Her research area is biomaterials.
Minghui Yang is professor at College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Central South University. His research areas include biomaterials and biosensors.
Xiaoqing Li is currently doing her Postdoc at the Department of Dermatology, Xiangya Hospital. Her research areas include electrochemical biosensors and fluorescence biosensors.
Xiang Chen is professor at the Department of Dermatology, Xiangya Hospital. His research areas focused on clinical and biomedical research associated with dermatology.
